one_line_takeaway: A 558-question, 44-CWE, 12-language benchmark with a Semgrep+LLM dual-judge shows leading LLMs produce secure code only ~37% of the time zero-shot, rising to ~61% with safety prompts and few-shot examples.
key_finding: State-of-the-art LLMs generate insecure code at high rates by default. Zero-shot average overall accuracy (fraction of secure outputs) is only 37.44%. Adding an explicit safety instruction raises the average by >20 points to 58.01%; adding few-shot insecure-code examples adds ~3 more points to 61.14%. Reasoning models consistently beat non-reasoning ones; o3 leads zero-shot at 46.42% and few-shot at 74.91%, while DeepSeek-R1 leads zero-shot+safety-instruction at 68.81%. The two judges are strongly complementary: on zero-shot data across all 13 models, both judges agreed 'secure' on 61.12% of samples, the LLM-Judge alone caught vulnerabilities SAST missed in 30.05%, SAST alone caught 6.24%, and both flagged only 2.59% — motivating the AND-of-judges design. SAST and LLM judges surface almost disjoint top-10 CWE lists (SAST catches injection/syntactic flaws like CWE-915, CWE-79, CWE-89; the LLM-Judge surfaces semantic/logic flaws like CWE-1104, CWE-778). By category, models are strongest on Memory Safety Violations (avg 76.16%) and weakest on Insecure Configuration (avg 12.50%).
core_contribution: SafeGenBench is a benchmark plus automatic evaluation framework for measuring the security (not functional correctness) of code produced by LLMs. The authors' own contributions are: (1) a curated dataset of 558 test questions spanning 12 programming languages and 44 distinct CWE types, organized into 8 high-level vulnerability categories, built via a three-stage pipeline (CWE taxonomy from OWASP Top-10 + CWE Top-25, LLM-generated test prompts that deliberately avoid mentioning security so as to mimic naive developer requests, and human expert annotation by 6 reviewers); (2) a dual-judge evaluation framework combining a Semgrep-based SAST scanner (broad, all-CWE syntactic scanning) with an LLM-judge (deep inspection of only the task-specified CWE), where code is scored secure (=1) only if BOTH judges pass; and (3) an empirical study of 13 LLMs from 6 institutions under 3 prompting settings.
threat_model: The concern is insecure code silently emitted by LLM coding assistants (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Trae-style tools) when developers request functionality without flagging security. Prompts are written to describe only intended behavior, so any resulting CWE-class flaw (e.g., SQL injection, hard-coded credentials, missing integrity checks) reflects the model's default security posture rather than adversarial prompting. This is a code-quality/secure-generation evaluation, not an attack or jailbreak paper; there is no active adversary manipulating the model.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) test cases are limited to single-function code generation, not project-level, multi-file, or multi-step tasks; (2) the framework only evaluates security, not whether the generated code actually fulfills the task (no functional-correctness check), so a trivially non-functional 'secure' answer could score well; (3) test questions are in Chinese, so results may be confounded by models' varying Chinese comprehension; (4) the judging relies on a single LLM-judge (DeepSeek-R1) and a single SAST tool (Semgrep), limiting robustness. Additional reviewer note: using DeepSeek-R1 as both an evaluated model and the unified judge risks self-preference bias; and the LLM-Judge only inspects the pre-specified CWE, so unprompted high-risk flaws depend entirely on SAST coverage.

datasets_used: SafeGenBench (this paper's own dataset): 558 test questions, 12 programming languages, 44 CWE types, 8 vulnerability categories
Vulnerability taxonomy sourced from OWASP Top-10 (2021) and CWE Top-25 Most Dangerous Software Weaknesses (2024)
Evaluation tool: Semgrep (SAST scanner); Pygments for language detection
quantitative_results: Zero-shot average overall accuracy across 13 models: 37.44%
Zero-shot + safety instruction average overall accuracy: 58.01% (>20 point gain)
Few-shot average overall accuracy: 61.14% (~3 point further gain)
Best zero-shot model: o3 at 46.42% overall
Best zero-shot+safety-instruction model: DeepSeek-R1 at 68.81% overall
Best few-shot model: o3 at 74.91% overall
Average SAST-Judge accuracy is high and low-variance (89.28% zero-shot, 90.75% +SI, 91.18% few-shot); LLM-Judge accuracy is lower and higher-variance (42.61% / 64.71% / 67.38%)

Category-wise zero-shot averages: Memory Safety Violations 76.16% (best), Code Injection & Execution 48.14%, Input Validation Flaws 34.88%, Session Management 32.17%, Insecure Data Management 31.28%, Authorization Flaws 24.07%, Resource Issues 14.69%, Insecure Configuration 12.50% (worst)
Dual-judge agreement (zero-shot, all models): both secure 61.12%, LLM-only catch 30.05%, SAST-only catch 6.24%, both vulnerable 2.59%
LLM-Judge reliability vs blind expert re-evaluation on a 9% stratified sample: 92% accuracy, 95% binomial CI [81.2%, 96.8%]
SAST top CWEs by frequency: CWE-915 2.62%, CWE-79 2.41%, CWE-601 1.41%, CWE-89 1.38%, CWE-78 1.13%
LLM-Judge lowest-accuracy CWEs: CWE-1104 8.79%, CWE-778 11.54%, CWE-223 16.67%, CWE-352 26.67%
baselines_compared: SafeGenBench positioned in Table 1 against prior code-security benchmarks: CodeLMSec (280 questions, 13 CWEs, 2 languages, SAST), CWEval (119 questions, 31 CWEs, 5 languages, outcome-driven), SecRepoBench (318 questions, 15 CWEs, 2 languages, OSS-Fuzz) — SafeGenBench is largest (558/44/13, SAST+LLM)

Evaluated LLMs (subjects, not baselines in the usual sense): closed-source GPT-4o, o1, o3-high, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Claude-3.7-Sonnet; open-source DeepSeek-V3, DeepSeek-R1, QwQ-32B, Qwen3-Dense (32B), Qwen3-MoE (235B-A22B), Llama4-Maverick, Llama4-Scout
Reasoning vs non-reasoning model comparison; three prompting strategies (zero-shot, zero-shot+safety-instruction, few-shot) as internal ablation
